True! I feel very nervous. I was very, very nervous before. Why do you say I am mad? The disease made my senses sharper and not dull. The most important thing was the strong sense of hearing. I heard everything in the sky and on the ground. I heard many sounds in a dark place. How can I be mad? Hearken! You can see how well I tell you the whole story. I cannot say how the idea came to me. Once I had it, it stayed in my mind all the time. There was no object there. There was no passion at all. I loved the old man very much. He never hurt me. He never gave me an insult. I do not want his gold. I think it was his eye. Yes, that was it! He has the eye of a vulture. It is a pale blue eye. The eye has a film over it. When I felt fear, my heart sank. I slowly decided to kill the old man. I wanted to be free of his eye forever. This is the main idea now. You think I am crazy. Crazy people do not know anything. You should see me now. You should see how wisely I worked. I used caution and foresight. I went to work with great care. I was very kind to the old man. I was kind all week. I did this before I killed him. Every night, around midnight, I opened his door gently. When I made a hole for my head, I used a dark lantern. It was closed so no light shone out. Then I put my head inside. You would laugh if you saw how cleverly I did it. I moved it
 slowly—very, very slowly, so that I might not disturb the old
 man’s sleep. It took me an hour to place my whole head within the
 opening so far that I could see him as he lay upon his bed. Ha! Can a madman be this wise? When my head was in the room, I opened the lantern carefully. The hinges made noise, so I opened it slowly. I let a thin ray of light fall on the vulture eye. I did this for seven long nights. Every night, I looked at midnight. I found the eye always closed. It was hard to do my work. It was not the old man who troubled me. It was his Evil Eye. Every morning, I went into the room. I spoke to him by name. I spoke in a warm tone. I asked him how he slept at night. He would have been a smart old man. He would think I watched him at twelve. I looked at him while he slept each night. On the eighth night, I was very careful when I opened the door. A watch’s minute hand moves more quickly than did mine. Never before that night had I felt the extent of my own powers—of
 my sagacity. I can hardly hold my feelings of joy. I opened the door slowly. He did not know my secret thoughts. I laughed at the idea. Maybe he heard me. He moved on the bed, startled. Now you might think I step back. But that is not true. His room was very dark and he could not see. The shutters were closed, so he felt scared. I pushed the door open slowly and carefully. I had my head in, and was about to open the lantern, when my
 thumb slipped upon the tin fastening, and the old man sprang up
 in bed, crying out—“Who’s there? ”

 I kept quite still and said nothing. I did not move at all for one hour. I did not hear him lie down. He sat up in bed and listened. I did the same every night. I heard the sounds in the wall. I heard a small groan. I knew it was a terrible sound. It was not a sound of pain. It was not a sound of grief. It is a low sound that comes from deep inside. It happens when people feel a great awe. I know the sound very well. Many nights, at midnight, the world sleeps. I feel a strong fear inside me. It grows louder and echoes in my mind. This sound makes me feel very scared. I say I know it well. I knew how the old man felt. I felt sorry for him. I smiled inside. I know he lies awake since the first small noise. He turned in bed that time. His fears grow all the time. They follow him everywhere he goes. He tried to like them for no reason, but he could not. He had been saying to himself—“It is nothing but the wind in
 the chimney—it is only a mouse crossing the floor,” or “It is
 merely a cricket which has made a single chirp. ” Yes, he had been
 trying to comfort himself with these suppositions: but he had
 found all in vain. Death comes close to him. His dark shadow surrounds the victim. All efforts are wasted. The shadow made him feel sad. He did not see or hear anything. He felt my head in the room. When I waited for a long time, I felt very patient. I did not hear him lie down. I decided to open a small part of the lantern. I opened it quietly. You cannot imagine how slowly I did this. Then a dim ray of light came from the crack. It fell right on the vulture's eye. It was open. I felt very angry when I looked at it. I saw it with perfect distinctness—all a dull blue, with a
 hideous veil over it that chilled the very marrow in my bones;
 but I could see nothing else of the old man’s face or person: for
 I had directed the ray as if by instinct, precisely upon the
 damned spot. And have I not told you that what you mistake for madness is but
 over-acuteness of the sense? Now, I hear a low and dull sound. It is quick, like a watch in cotton. I knew that sound very well. It was the beating of the
 old man’s heart. It increased my fury, as the beating of a drum
 stimulates the soldier into courage. But I still stayed quiet and did not speak. I barely took a breath. I held the lantern still. I wanted to see how steady I can hold the light on my eye. The loud beating of the heart got faster. It grew faster and louder every moment. The old man’s terror must have been
 extreme! It grew louder, I say, louder every moment! Do you see me clearly? I tell you I am nervous. I feel this way now. At night, the old house is very quiet. I hear a strange noise. This noise makes me feel scared. I cannot control my fear. I stayed still for a few more minutes. The beating got louder and louder. I think the heart will burst. A new worry took hold of me. The sound could reach a neighbor. The old man’s hour had come! With a loud yell, I
 threw open the lantern and leaped into the room. He shrieked one time. It was the only time. I quickly pulled him to the floor. I then moved the heavy bed over him. I smiled happily because the work was almost finished. The heart beats for many minutes with a soft sound. This did not bother me. It was not loud enough to hear. Finally, it stopped. The old man is dead. I took away the bed. I looked at the body. Yes, he was very still. He was dead. I put my hand on my heart. I held it there for many minutes. There is no pulsing. He was very still. He did not move at all. His eye does not bother me anymore. If you still think I am mad, you will not think so anymore. I will tell you about the smart things I did. These things helped me hide the body well. The night was ending. I worked quickly and in silence. First, I cut the body into pieces. I cut off the head. I cut off the arms. I cut off the legs. I then picked up three wooden boards from the floor. I placed them between the beams. I replaced the boards with great care. No one could see anything wrong. There is nothing to wash out. There is no stain at all. There are no blood spots anywhere. I was very careful because of that. A tub caught everything. It was surprising and funny! ha! When I had made an end of these labors, it was four o’clock—still
 dark as midnight. As the bell sounded the hour, there came a
 knocking at the street door. I went down to open it. I felt light and happy. I had nothing to fear now. Three men came in. They introduced themselves as police officers. A neighbor hears a loud scream during the night. This raises some suspicion. They give information to the police. The police come to search the place. I smiled. I had nothing to fear. I welcomed the men. I said the loud yell was my own voice in a dream. The old man was not here. He was in the country. I showed my visitors different rooms in the house. I asked them to search. I asked them to search well. I took them to his room. I showed them his treasures. They were safe and untouched. I feel confident and bring chairs into the room. I want them here to rest from work. I sit down on the spot above the victim. The officers feel happy. My way of talking won them over. I felt calm and relaxed. They sat down. I answered happily. They talked about things they knew. Soon, I felt pale and wanted them to leave. My head hurt. I thought I heard a ringing in my ears. They sat and talked. The ringing was clear and loud. I talked more freely to feel better. But the ringing did not stop. It grew stronger until I realized the noise was real. I became very pale. I talked more clearly and with a loud voice. The sound became louder. I did not know what to do. It was a low and dull sound. It was a quick noise. It sounded like a watch in cotton. I gasped for breath. The officers did not hear me. I talked quickly and with strong feelings. The noise got louder and louder. I woke up and talked about small things. I spoke loudly and moved my hands. The noise got louder and louder. "Why are they still here?" I walked back and forth on the floor. I felt angry when I saw the men. The noise grew louder and louder. Oh God! What can I do? I felt very angry. I shouted and made noise. I used bad words. I moved the chair where I sat before. I made noise on the floor. The noise was loud and got louder. It grew louder and louder. The men talk and smile happily. Did they not hear anything? "God is great!" —no, no! They heard something. It was loud and surprising. They thought something was wrong. They knew what was happening. They laughed at my fear and made fun of me. I thought this before, and I think this now. But anything feels better than this pain. Anything feels better than this mocking. I can no longer stand those fake smiles. I felt very scared. I wanted to scream or die. And now it happens again! —hark! louder! louder! louder! _louder! _

 “Villains! ” I shrieked, “dissemble no more! I say I did it. "Please pull up the wooden boards." "Look here. Look here!" It is the sound of his ugly heart.
